channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #440
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Government Rejects Calls for Stricter HMO Regulations

Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook has dismissed petitions requesting additional legislation to curb the conversion of family homes into Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs). The government maintains that local councils already possess adequate powers, including Article 4 Directions, to manage HMO concentrations where necessary. Critics argue that rising HMO numbers contribute to parking pressures, anti-social behaviour and strain on local services. Homeowners concerned about HMO proliferation in their area may wish to contact their local planning authority directly.

New Guidance for Landlords on Pet-Friendly Lets

The Dogs Trust has published recommendations for landlords navigating the recent changes under the Renters' Reform Act, which prevents blanket bans on pets. Key considerations include ensuring suitable living conditions for animals, such as adequate space, resting areas and safety measures like baby gates. The guidance notes that multiple pet households require individual assessment, factoring in the property layout and owner's experience. In one reported case, tenant advocacy group Advocat successfully challenged a landlord attempting to impose unreasonable pet-related conditions, highlighting the importance of compliance with the new legislation.

Informal Lets to Friends and Family Carry Risks

A Hiscox survey reveals 63% of landlords have rented to acquaintances, with 18% doing so without formal agreements. Industry experts warn that even lettings to friends or relatives remain subject to full regulatory requirements. Informal arrangements may lead to significant legal and financial consequences, including difficulties regaining possession or facing penalties for non-compliance. Property owners might consider professional advice before entering into any rental agreement, regardless of the tenant's relationship to them.

As the private rental sector continues to evolve, staying informed about legislative changes and best practices remains crucial for all landlords. Those with concerns about specific situations may find it helpful to consult official government guidance or seek independent legal advice.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Government Rejects Calls for Stricter HMO Regulations

Housing Minister Matthew Pennycook has dismissed petitions requesting additional legislation to curb the conversion of family homes into Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs). The government maintains that local councils already possess adequate powers, including Article 4 Directions, to manage HMO concentrations where necessary. Critics argue that rising HMO numbers contribute to parking pressures, anti-social behaviour and strain on local services. Homeowners concerned about HMO proliferation in their area may wish to contact their local planning authority directly.

New Guidance for Landlords on Pet-Friendly Lets

The Dogs Trust has published recommendations for landlords navigating the recent changes under the Renters' Reform Act, which prevents blanket bans on pets. Key considerations include ensuring suitable living conditions for animals, such as adequate space, resting areas and safety measures like baby gates. The guidance notes that multiple pet households require individual assessment, factoring in the property layout and owner's experience. In one reported case, tenant advocacy group Advocat successfully challenged a landlord attempting to impose unreasonable pet-related conditions, highlighting the importance of compliance with the new legislation.

Informal Lets to Friends and Family Carry Risks

A Hiscox survey reveals 63% of landlords have rented to acquaintances, with 18% doing so without formal agreements. Industry experts warn that even lettings to friends or relatives remain subject to full regulatory requirements. Informal arrangements may lead to significant legal and financial consequences, including difficulties regaining possession or facing penalties for non-compliance. Property owners might consider professional advice before entering into any rental agreement, regardless of the tenant's relationship to them.

As the private rental sector continues to evolve, staying informed about legislative changes and best practices remains crucial for all landlords. Those with concerns about specific situations may find it helpful to consult official government guidance or seek independent legal advice.

item.title: Why Overseas Property Buyers Need More Than Pretty Listing Photos
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Importance of Due Diligence for Overseas Property Buyers

Purchasing property abroad presents exciting opportunities but also unique challenges. Whilst attractive listing photos may initially draw buyers in, those investing in overseas real estate often require far more comprehensive information before committing to such a significant financial decision.

Unlike domestic purchases where buyers can easily visit properties in person, overseas investors frequently rely on digital representations alone. This makes thorough due diligence particularly crucial to avoid unexpected issues that might only become apparent during an in-person viewing.

Key Considerations Beyond Property Imagery

Prospective international buyers might consider several important factors that extend beyond appealing photographs:

  • Local market conditions and property valuation trends
  • Legal frameworks and ownership regulations in the target country
  • Currency exchange risks and international payment processes
  • Tax implications both abroad and in the UK
  • Ongoing maintenance and property management options

Virtual tours and detailed video walkthroughs could provide more comprehensive property insights than static images alone. However, even these may not reveal structural issues or neighbourhood characteristics that become apparent during physical inspections.

Mitigating Risks in Cross-Border Transactions

International property purchases inherently carry additional complexities compared to domestic transactions. Buyers may wish to explore professional services that can help navigate these challenges:

  • Independent property valuation services
  • Local legal experts specialising in real estate
  • Reputable international surveyors
  • Currency exchange specialists
  • Bilingual estate agents with cross-border experience

Whilst these services represent additional costs, they might prove valuable in preventing expensive mistakes or uncovering issues that could affect the property's long-term value.

For those considering overseas property investment, comprehensive research and professional guidance could make the difference between a successful international purchase and a problematic transaction. The most attractive listing photos rarely tell the full story, making thorough due diligence essential for cross-border buyers.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Importance of Due Diligence for Overseas Property Buyers

Purchasing property abroad presents exciting opportunities but also unique challenges. Whilst attractive listing photos may initially draw buyers in, those investing in overseas real estate often require far more comprehensive information before committing to such a significant financial decision.

Unlike domestic purchases where buyers can easily visit properties in person, overseas investors frequently rely on digital representations alone. This makes thorough due diligence particularly crucial to avoid unexpected issues that might only become apparent during an in-person viewing.

Key Considerations Beyond Property Imagery

Prospective international buyers might consider several important factors that extend beyond appealing photographs:

  • Local market conditions and property valuation trends
  • Legal frameworks and ownership regulations in the target country
  • Currency exchange risks and international payment processes
  • Tax implications both abroad and in the UK
  • Ongoing maintenance and property management options

Virtual tours and detailed video walkthroughs could provide more comprehensive property insights than static images alone. However, even these may not reveal structural issues or neighbourhood characteristics that become apparent during physical inspections.

Mitigating Risks in Cross-Border Transactions

International property purchases inherently carry additional complexities compared to domestic transactions. Buyers may wish to explore professional services that can help navigate these challenges:

  • Independent property valuation services
  • Local legal experts specialising in real estate
  • Reputable international surveyors
  • Currency exchange specialists
  • Bilingual estate agents with cross-border experience

Whilst these services represent additional costs, they might prove valuable in preventing expensive mistakes or uncovering issues that could affect the property's long-term value.

For those considering overseas property investment, comprehensive research and professional guidance could make the difference between a successful international purchase and a problematic transaction. The most attractive listing photos rarely tell the full story, making thorough due diligence essential for cross-border buyers.

item.title: Warning signs for UK housing market as property sales slump
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has shown signs of cooling in early June, with agreed sales falling nearly 9% compared to the same period last year. Recent data reveals regional variations, with London and Northern Ireland experiencing the most pronounced slowdowns, whilst Scotland and Yorkshire & Humber demonstrated relative stability. These figures suggest a loss of momentum following a stronger start to 2026.

Regional variations in market activity

Property sales agreed during the first week of June presented a mixed picture across the UK. Northern Ireland saw the steepest decline at 48.75%, followed by Outer London (18.45%) and Inner London (13.12%). The South West and North East also recorded double-digit decreases in transactions. In contrast, Scotland's market remained virtually unchanged with just a 0.6% dip, and Yorkshire & Humber saw a modest 2.06% reduction.

Whilst the overall trend points to softening demand, the number of new property listings tells a different story. Week 22 of 2026 saw 38,900 new listings – above both the 2026 weekly average (37,400) and the 10-year average for this period (30,900). Year-to-date figures show listings running 0.5% ahead of 2025 and 5.9% higher than 2024 levels.

Contextualising the slowdown

Market analysts note that whilst the year-on-year comparison shows a decline, the figures must be viewed in context. The 2025 data being compared against followed an unusually active spring market, whilst 2026 has seen more typical seasonal patterns. The current weekly average of 24,800 sales agreed remains slightly above the 10-year average of 23,500 for this period.

Several factors could be influencing the current market dynamics:

  • Potential buyers awaiting clearer economic signals
  • Seasonal variations in market activity
  • Regional affordability pressures, particularly in London
  • The impact of recent mortgage rate fluctuations

For homeowners considering selling, the relatively high number of new listings suggests competition may be increasing in some areas. Borrowers might find it valuable to monitor local market conditions and seek independent mortgage advice to understand how these trends could affect their options.

As the summer market develops, industry observers will be watching to see whether this represents a temporary adjustment or the beginning of a more sustained slowdown. The coming weeks' data may provide clearer indications of the market's direction for the remainder of 2026.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has shown signs of cooling in early June, with agreed sales falling nearly 9% compared to the same period last year. Recent data reveals regional variations, with London and Northern Ireland experiencing the most pronounced slowdowns, whilst Scotland and Yorkshire & Humber demonstrated relative stability. These figures suggest a loss of momentum following a stronger start to 2026.

Regional variations in market activity

Property sales agreed during the first week of June presented a mixed picture across the UK. Northern Ireland saw the steepest decline at 48.75%, followed by Outer London (18.45%) and Inner London (13.12%). The South West and North East also recorded double-digit decreases in transactions. In contrast, Scotland's market remained virtually unchanged with just a 0.6% dip, and Yorkshire & Humber saw a modest 2.06% reduction.

Whilst the overall trend points to softening demand, the number of new property listings tells a different story. Week 22 of 2026 saw 38,900 new listings – above both the 2026 weekly average (37,400) and the 10-year average for this period (30,900). Year-to-date figures show listings running 0.5% ahead of 2025 and 5.9% higher than 2024 levels.

Contextualising the slowdown

Market analysts note that whilst the year-on-year comparison shows a decline, the figures must be viewed in context. The 2025 data being compared against followed an unusually active spring market, whilst 2026 has seen more typical seasonal patterns. The current weekly average of 24,800 sales agreed remains slightly above the 10-year average of 23,500 for this period.

Several factors could be influencing the current market dynamics:

  • Potential buyers awaiting clearer economic signals
  • Seasonal variations in market activity
  • Regional affordability pressures, particularly in London
  • The impact of recent mortgage rate fluctuations

For homeowners considering selling, the relatively high number of new listings suggests competition may be increasing in some areas. Borrowers might find it valuable to monitor local market conditions and seek independent mortgage advice to understand how these trends could affect their options.

As the summer market develops, industry observers will be watching to see whether this represents a temporary adjustment or the beginning of a more sustained slowdown. The coming weeks' data may provide clearer indications of the market's direction for the remainder of 2026.

item.title: Fast-growing property firm swoops for long-established agency
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Dacre, Son & Hartley, a well-established Yorkshire estate agency, has expanded its lettings division with the acquisition of Craven Homelet Services, a Settle-based firm with over three decades of experience in North Yorkshire’s rental market. Founded in 1988, Craven Homelet manages a diverse portfolio of properties across North Craven and Ribblesdale, including the picturesque Yorkshire Dales. This move strengthens Dacre, Son & Hartley’s presence in the region and aligns with its broader growth strategy in the lettings sector.

A Strategic Expansion

This acquisition marks the latest step in Dacre, Son & Hartley’s ambitious growth plans, which include expanding its lettings and property management operations. Earlier this year, the firm relaunched its lettings division, followed by the acquisition of Lister Haigh, a North Yorkshire estate agency and rural consultancy, in 2023. These strategic moves have significantly boosted the number of rental properties under its management, doubling its portfolio over the past 12 months.

Craven Homelet’s owner, Robert Senior, will join Dacre, Son & Hartley as part of the deal, bringing his extensive expertise to lead lettings and property management services in the Settle area. The acquisition also expands the firm’s specialist lettings team to eight members, further enhancing its ability to meet growing landlord and tenant demand.

Building on Local Expertise

Craven Homelet’s long-standing reputation in the local rental market makes it a valuable addition to Dacre, Son & Hartley’s network. With nearly four decades of experience, the firm has managed a wide range of properties across Ribblesdale and the Three Peaks area, earning the trust of landlords and tenants alike. Robert Senior’s deep knowledge of the market and strong relationships within the community are expected to bolster Dacre, Son & Hartley’s commitment to delivering high-quality services.

Patrick McCutcheon, head of residential at Dacre, Son & Hartley, highlighted the significance of the acquisition, stating, “Craven Homelet’s excellent track record and Robert’s expertise make this a natural fit for our business. This move allows us to continue providing consistent, professional support to landlords and tenants while expanding our footprint in the region.”

Meeting Market Challenges

The acquisition comes at a pivotal time for the rental sector, with the recent introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act increasing regulatory and compliance requirements for landlords. As tenant demand remains exceptionally high, many landlords are seeking expert support to navigate these changes. Robert Senior emphasised the importance of this development, noting, “Joining Dacres allows us to leverage their extensive resources and marketing reach, ensuring we can meet the evolving needs of landlords and tenants in this dynamic market.”

With over 200 years of history and a network of 17 offices across North and West Yorkshire, Dacre, Son & Hartley is well-positioned to continue its growth trajectory. This latest acquisition underscores the firm’s commitment to strengthening its lettings division and supporting clients in an increasingly complex rental landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Dacre, Son & Hartley, a well-established Yorkshire estate agency, has expanded its lettings division with the acquisition of Craven Homelet Services, a Settle-based firm with over three decades of experience in North Yorkshire’s rental market. Founded in 1988, Craven Homelet manages a diverse portfolio of properties across North Craven and Ribblesdale, including the picturesque Yorkshire Dales. This move strengthens Dacre, Son & Hartley’s presence in the region and aligns with its broader growth strategy in the lettings sector.

A Strategic Expansion

This acquisition marks the latest step in Dacre, Son & Hartley’s ambitious growth plans, which include expanding its lettings and property management operations. Earlier this year, the firm relaunched its lettings division, followed by the acquisition of Lister Haigh, a North Yorkshire estate agency and rural consultancy, in 2023. These strategic moves have significantly boosted the number of rental properties under its management, doubling its portfolio over the past 12 months.

Craven Homelet’s owner, Robert Senior, will join Dacre, Son & Hartley as part of the deal, bringing his extensive expertise to lead lettings and property management services in the Settle area. The acquisition also expands the firm’s specialist lettings team to eight members, further enhancing its ability to meet growing landlord and tenant demand.

Building on Local Expertise

Craven Homelet’s long-standing reputation in the local rental market makes it a valuable addition to Dacre, Son & Hartley’s network. With nearly four decades of experience, the firm has managed a wide range of properties across Ribblesdale and the Three Peaks area, earning the trust of landlords and tenants alike. Robert Senior’s deep knowledge of the market and strong relationships within the community are expected to bolster Dacre, Son & Hartley’s commitment to delivering high-quality services.

Patrick McCutcheon, head of residential at Dacre, Son & Hartley, highlighted the significance of the acquisition, stating, “Craven Homelet’s excellent track record and Robert’s expertise make this a natural fit for our business. This move allows us to continue providing consistent, professional support to landlords and tenants while expanding our footprint in the region.”

Meeting Market Challenges

The acquisition comes at a pivotal time for the rental sector, with the recent introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act increasing regulatory and compliance requirements for landlords. As tenant demand remains exceptionally high, many landlords are seeking expert support to navigate these changes. Robert Senior emphasised the importance of this development, noting, “Joining Dacres allows us to leverage their extensive resources and marketing reach, ensuring we can meet the evolving needs of landlords and tenants in this dynamic market.”

With over 200 years of history and a network of 17 offices across North and West Yorkshire, Dacre, Son & Hartley is well-positioned to continue its growth trajectory. This latest acquisition underscores the firm’s commitment to strengthening its lettings division and supporting clients in an increasingly complex rental landscape.

item.title: Summer webinar programme to tackle key conveyancing issues
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Conveyancing Association (CA) has unveiled a new summer webinar initiative designed to help legal professionals navigate pressing challenges in property transactions. Running from June through August, the programme will address operational efficiencies, regulatory updates and emerging business considerations for conveyancing practices.

Key Topics for Property Professionals

The webinar series kicks off on 24th June with a session on automating administrative processes, delivered in collaboration with Post Partner. This will explore streamlining document handling and post allocation systems to reduce workloads. A follow-up session planned for 7th July, developed with Move Reports, will examine stamp duty complexities and new tax registration requirements affecting law firms.

Further instalments throughout the summer will cover:

  • Technological advancements in conveyancing
  • Regulatory compliance updates
  • Client service improvements
  • Professional service innovations

Addressing Industry Changes

Nicky Heathcote, the CA's non-executive chair, highlighted how the initiative taps into affiliate members' expertise to help firms adapt. "The conveyancing sector faces rapid changes - from new regulations to evolving client expectations," she noted. "These webinars aim to equip practices with actionable insights from industry leaders."

The association plans to announce additional webinar dates and topics in coming weeks, with all sessions available exclusively to CA members. Legal professionals looking to stay ahead of sector developments may wish to explore this professional development opportunity as the property market enters its traditionally busier summer period.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Conveyancing Association (CA) has unveiled a new summer webinar initiative designed to help legal professionals navigate pressing challenges in property transactions. Running from June through August, the programme will address operational efficiencies, regulatory updates and emerging business considerations for conveyancing practices.

Key Topics for Property Professionals

The webinar series kicks off on 24th June with a session on automating administrative processes, delivered in collaboration with Post Partner. This will explore streamlining document handling and post allocation systems to reduce workloads. A follow-up session planned for 7th July, developed with Move Reports, will examine stamp duty complexities and new tax registration requirements affecting law firms.

Further instalments throughout the summer will cover:

  • Technological advancements in conveyancing
  • Regulatory compliance updates
  • Client service improvements
  • Professional service innovations

Addressing Industry Changes

Nicky Heathcote, the CA's non-executive chair, highlighted how the initiative taps into affiliate members' expertise to help firms adapt. "The conveyancing sector faces rapid changes - from new regulations to evolving client expectations," she noted. "These webinars aim to equip practices with actionable insights from industry leaders."

The association plans to announce additional webinar dates and topics in coming weeks, with all sessions available exclusively to CA members. Legal professionals looking to stay ahead of sector developments may wish to explore this professional development opportunity as the property market enters its traditionally busier summer period.

item.title: Seaside hotspots see property prices tumble, yet buyers still pay hefty premiums
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Seaside property prices dip but premiums persist in sought-after locations

New research reveals significant price corrections in several UK coastal property hotspots over the past year, though many desirable seaside towns continue to command substantial premiums over their inland counterparts. The analysis from estate agency Yopa shows double-digit percentage declines across multiple locations, with Northumberland's Seahouses recording the steepest fall at 38% year-on-year.

Market adjustments after pandemic boom

The data highlights how previously booming coastal markets are undergoing adjustments following exceptional pandemic-era growth. Alongside Seahouses, notable declines include:

  • Portmeirion, Wales: 20.2% decrease
  • Fowey, Cornwall: 18.3% drop
  • North Berwick: 13.8% reduction
  • Lossiemouth: 11.8% fall

Yopa's CEO Verona Frankish suggests these corrections reflect normalisation after the unprecedented demand surge during lockdowns, when remote working flexibility drove many buyers to prioritise coastal lifestyle locations.

Premium pricing persists in prime locations

Despite these downward adjustments, numerous coastal destinations maintain significant price premiums compared to their surrounding areas:

  • Sandbanks, Dorset: 75.6% above Bournemouth average (£682,598)
  • Padstow: 70% premium over Cornwall average
  • Salcombe: 68.1% higher than local market

Several other locations including Perranporth, Southwold and Porthcawl continue to command premiums exceeding 50% over their regional averages, demonstrating enduring demand for prime coastal properties.

Affordable alternatives emerge

The research also identifies coastal towns where property values sit substantially below regional averages, potentially offering budget-conscious buyers attractive opportunities:

  • Thurso, Highlands: 38.1% below area average
  • Scarborough: 34.8% under North Yorkshire norm
  • Weston-super-Mare: 27.5% discount

This polarised market landscape suggests coastal property buyers might benefit from carefully comparing locations, as substantial price variations exist between different seaside destinations across the UK.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Seaside property prices dip but premiums persist in sought-after locations

New research reveals significant price corrections in several UK coastal property hotspots over the past year, though many desirable seaside towns continue to command substantial premiums over their inland counterparts. The analysis from estate agency Yopa shows double-digit percentage declines across multiple locations, with Northumberland's Seahouses recording the steepest fall at 38% year-on-year.

Market adjustments after pandemic boom

The data highlights how previously booming coastal markets are undergoing adjustments following exceptional pandemic-era growth. Alongside Seahouses, notable declines include:

  • Portmeirion, Wales: 20.2% decrease
  • Fowey, Cornwall: 18.3% drop
  • North Berwick: 13.8% reduction
  • Lossiemouth: 11.8% fall

Yopa's CEO Verona Frankish suggests these corrections reflect normalisation after the unprecedented demand surge during lockdowns, when remote working flexibility drove many buyers to prioritise coastal lifestyle locations.

Premium pricing persists in prime locations

Despite these downward adjustments, numerous coastal destinations maintain significant price premiums compared to their surrounding areas:

  • Sandbanks, Dorset: 75.6% above Bournemouth average (£682,598)
  • Padstow: 70% premium over Cornwall average
  • Salcombe: 68.1% higher than local market

Several other locations including Perranporth, Southwold and Porthcawl continue to command premiums exceeding 50% over their regional averages, demonstrating enduring demand for prime coastal properties.

Affordable alternatives emerge

The research also identifies coastal towns where property values sit substantially below regional averages, potentially offering budget-conscious buyers attractive opportunities:

  • Thurso, Highlands: 38.1% below area average
  • Scarborough: 34.8% under North Yorkshire norm
  • Weston-super-Mare: 27.5% discount

This polarised market landscape suggests coastal property buyers might benefit from carefully comparing locations, as substantial price variations exist between different seaside destinations across the UK.

item.title: MPs call on Reeves to scrap ‘economy-damaging’ stamp duty
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

MPs Urge Stamp Duty Reform to Boost Housing Market

A cross-party group of MPs has called for an urgent review of stamp duty, describing the tax as a barrier to homeownership and a drag on economic growth. The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee argues the current system reduces housing market mobility whilst failing to address affordability challenges. Their report suggests the Treasury should explore alternatives that could stimulate transactions without reducing tax revenues.

How Stamp Duty Impacts Buyers and the Market

The committee highlighted several concerns about the current stamp duty system:

  • Discourages downsizing and relocation, limiting housing stock availability
  • Creates additional costs for first-time buyers already facing deposit challenges
  • Distorts the market through temporary relief schemes that create boom-bust cycles
  • Fails to reflect regional property price variations under current thresholds

MPs particularly criticised the pandemic-era stamp duty holiday, which temporarily boosted transactions but contributed to price inflation before demand collapsed when the scheme ended. The report warns against similar short-term fixes, recommending instead a fundamental review of property taxation.

Potential Solutions Under Consideration

The committee has proposed several approaches the government might consider:

  • A revenue-neutral replacement tax that doesn't penalise mobility
  • Regional band adjustments to better reflect local market conditions
  • Integration with wider property tax reforms, including council tax
  • Permanent reliefs targeted at specific groups like first-time buyers

With homeownership rates declining over two decades, policymakers face growing pressure to address housing affordability. The report stresses that any reforms should form part of a long-term strategy rather than offering temporary relief that could distort the market.

As the debate continues, homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor developments, as significant stamp duty changes could affect transaction costs and market dynamics in the coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

MPs Urge Stamp Duty Reform to Boost Housing Market

A cross-party group of MPs has called for an urgent review of stamp duty, describing the tax as a barrier to homeownership and a drag on economic growth. The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee argues the current system reduces housing market mobility whilst failing to address affordability challenges. Their report suggests the Treasury should explore alternatives that could stimulate transactions without reducing tax revenues.

How Stamp Duty Impacts Buyers and the Market

The committee highlighted several concerns about the current stamp duty system:

  • Discourages downsizing and relocation, limiting housing stock availability
  • Creates additional costs for first-time buyers already facing deposit challenges
  • Distorts the market through temporary relief schemes that create boom-bust cycles
  • Fails to reflect regional property price variations under current thresholds

MPs particularly criticised the pandemic-era stamp duty holiday, which temporarily boosted transactions but contributed to price inflation before demand collapsed when the scheme ended. The report warns against similar short-term fixes, recommending instead a fundamental review of property taxation.

Potential Solutions Under Consideration

The committee has proposed several approaches the government might consider:

  • A revenue-neutral replacement tax that doesn't penalise mobility
  • Regional band adjustments to better reflect local market conditions
  • Integration with wider property tax reforms, including council tax
  • Permanent reliefs targeted at specific groups like first-time buyers

With homeownership rates declining over two decades, policymakers face growing pressure to address housing affordability. The report stresses that any reforms should form part of a long-term strategy rather than offering temporary relief that could distort the market.

As the debate continues, homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor developments, as significant stamp duty changes could affect transaction costs and market dynamics in the coming years.

item.title: Case updates from your lawyer – useful or not?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Why Regular Case Updates Matter in Conveyancing

A recent report examining common complaints made to the Legal Ombudsman has highlighted communication gaps as a key frustration for clients during property transactions. Whilst conveyancing delays are often unavoidable, many homeowners express dissatisfaction when left in the dark about progress – or lack thereof – on their case.

The findings suggest that even simple updates confirming no developments have occurred could significantly improve client satisfaction. Borrowers awaiting mortgage approvals or property completions might appreciate periodic check-ins from their solicitor, rather than enduring radio silence between major milestones.

The Communication Challenge in Property Transactions

Conveyancing remains a frequent pain point in the homebuying process, with many factors contributing to delays:

  • Chain dependencies beyond any single solicitor's control
  • Backlogs at local authorities for searches
  • Capacity pressures at busy law firms

However, the research indicates that transparency about these hurdles could help manage expectations. Homeowners facing weeks without updates might consider politely requesting a communication schedule from their conveyancer at the outset. Some firms now offer client portals providing real-time case tracking.

For mortgage applicants particularly, understanding where their application sits in the process could reduce anxiety. Lenders and brokers might also play a role in setting realistic timelines for different transaction types.

Balancing Communication With Productivity

Legal professionals face a delicate balancing act between client communication and progressing cases efficiently. Frequent unnecessary updates could potentially:

  • Increase administrative burdens
  • Slow down actual casework
  • Generate follow-up queries

That said, establishing clear communication protocols early in the solicitor-client relationship could benefit both parties. Homebuyers may wish to discuss preferred update methods (email, text or portal) and frequency during initial consultations.

As the property market continues to face pressures, maintaining open channels between all parties – buyers, sellers, agents and legal professionals – remains crucial for smoother transactions. While no magic solution exists for conveyancing delays, proactive communication could help manage frustrations throughout the process.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Why Regular Case Updates Matter in Conveyancing

A recent report examining common complaints made to the Legal Ombudsman has highlighted communication gaps as a key frustration for clients during property transactions. Whilst conveyancing delays are often unavoidable, many homeowners express dissatisfaction when left in the dark about progress – or lack thereof – on their case.

The findings suggest that even simple updates confirming no developments have occurred could significantly improve client satisfaction. Borrowers awaiting mortgage approvals or property completions might appreciate periodic check-ins from their solicitor, rather than enduring radio silence between major milestones.

The Communication Challenge in Property Transactions

Conveyancing remains a frequent pain point in the homebuying process, with many factors contributing to delays:

  • Chain dependencies beyond any single solicitor's control
  • Backlogs at local authorities for searches
  • Capacity pressures at busy law firms

However, the research indicates that transparency about these hurdles could help manage expectations. Homeowners facing weeks without updates might consider politely requesting a communication schedule from their conveyancer at the outset. Some firms now offer client portals providing real-time case tracking.

For mortgage applicants particularly, understanding where their application sits in the process could reduce anxiety. Lenders and brokers might also play a role in setting realistic timelines for different transaction types.

Balancing Communication With Productivity

Legal professionals face a delicate balancing act between client communication and progressing cases efficiently. Frequent unnecessary updates could potentially:

  • Increase administrative burdens
  • Slow down actual casework
  • Generate follow-up queries

That said, establishing clear communication protocols early in the solicitor-client relationship could benefit both parties. Homebuyers may wish to discuss preferred update methods (email, text or portal) and frequency during initial consultations.

As the property market continues to face pressures, maintaining open channels between all parties – buyers, sellers, agents and legal professionals – remains crucial for smoother transactions. While no magic solution exists for conveyancing delays, proactive communication could help manage frustrations throughout the process.

item.title: The government’s 1.5m new homes target? ‘Nonsense’, says shadow housing secretary
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government's pledge to build 1.5 million new homes has been dismissed as "nonsense" by shadow housing secretary James Cleverly, who argues that ministers are prioritising headline-grabbing targets over practical solutions to boost housing supply. Speaking on a recent industry podcast, the Conservative MP warned that simply repeating housing targets would not translate into more homes being built without addressing fundamental challenges in the construction sector.

Call for greater focus on construction sector support

Cleverly highlighted what he sees as a growing disconnect between political ambition and actual housing delivery. The shadow minister suggested policymakers should shift their attention towards supporting the businesses and tradespeople responsible for building homes, rather than focusing solely on numerical targets.

He told listeners: "One of the problems with politics is everybody wants to be an architect. No one wants to be a builder. Bright ideas are valuable, but someone has to put them into practice." The MP emphasised the need to recognise and reward those delivering housing projects, warning that without this approach, targets would remain unmet.

Skills shortage and stamp duty reform

The discussion turned to long-standing challenges in the construction industry, with Cleverly arguing that the UK has undervalued vocational skills for decades. He suggested this cultural attitude has contributed to labour shortages that now hinder housebuilding efforts.

The shadow secretary also outlined Conservative proposals to scrap stamp duty, describing it as a "drag anchor on the British economy" that affects both homebuyers' finances and their willingness to move. He noted how housing transactions often stimulate additional economic activity through home improvements and renovations, typically benefiting local tradespeople rather than large construction firms.

As the housing debate continues, Cleverly's comments highlight ongoing concerns about whether current policies adequately address the practical barriers to increasing housing supply. With construction skills shortages and economic factors like stamp duty influencing the market, homeowners and prospective buyers might watch this space for further policy developments.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The government's pledge to build 1.5 million new homes has been dismissed as "nonsense" by shadow housing secretary James Cleverly, who argues that ministers are prioritising headline-grabbing targets over practical solutions to boost housing supply. Speaking on a recent industry podcast, the Conservative MP warned that simply repeating housing targets would not translate into more homes being built without addressing fundamental challenges in the construction sector.

Call for greater focus on construction sector support

Cleverly highlighted what he sees as a growing disconnect between political ambition and actual housing delivery. The shadow minister suggested policymakers should shift their attention towards supporting the businesses and tradespeople responsible for building homes, rather than focusing solely on numerical targets.

He told listeners: "One of the problems with politics is everybody wants to be an architect. No one wants to be a builder. Bright ideas are valuable, but someone has to put them into practice." The MP emphasised the need to recognise and reward those delivering housing projects, warning that without this approach, targets would remain unmet.

Skills shortage and stamp duty reform

The discussion turned to long-standing challenges in the construction industry, with Cleverly arguing that the UK has undervalued vocational skills for decades. He suggested this cultural attitude has contributed to labour shortages that now hinder housebuilding efforts.

The shadow secretary also outlined Conservative proposals to scrap stamp duty, describing it as a "drag anchor on the British economy" that affects both homebuyers' finances and their willingness to move. He noted how housing transactions often stimulate additional economic activity through home improvements and renovations, typically benefiting local tradespeople rather than large construction firms.

As the housing debate continues, Cleverly's comments highlight ongoing concerns about whether current policies adequately address the practical barriers to increasing housing supply. With construction skills shortages and economic factors like stamp duty influencing the market, homeowners and prospective buyers might watch this space for further policy developments.

item.title: Estate agents remain biggest source of conveyancing referrals
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Estate Agents Still Lead in Conveyancing Referrals

New research highlights estate agents' enduring influence over homebuyers' choice of conveyancing services, with nearly a third of purchasers selecting solicitors based on agent recommendations. A survey of 1,208 recent homeowners found 32% appointed conveyancers referred by their estate agent, making this the most common route to instruction. The findings emerge as some housing markets experience softer demand, intensifying competition among conveyancing firms for new business.

How Buyers Choose Their Conveyancer

Beyond estate agent referrals, personal recommendations from friends and family ranked as the second most popular method (25%), whilst only 19% of buyers found their conveyancer through online searches. Interestingly, recommendations appear to outweigh cost considerations for many purchasers. Among those comparing multiple firms, 38% prioritised professional referrals over other factors, compared to 20% who focused primarily on price and 11% swayed by online reviews.

The Importance of Agent Relationships

The research underscores how estate agents remain pivotal in the conveyancing process, even as digital comparison tools grow in popularity. Industry experts suggest these findings demonstrate why conveyancing firms might benefit from nurturing strong relationships with local agents. As one solicitor noted, agents typically recommend firms they trust to provide efficient, reliable service that reflects well on their own business and helps transactions proceed smoothly – particularly valuable in today's more challenging market conditions.

With estate agent recommendations continuing to shape consumer decisions significantly, conveyancers may wish to consider how maintaining these professional relationships could support their business growth amidst fluctuating market demand.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Estate Agents Still Lead in Conveyancing Referrals

New research highlights estate agents' enduring influence over homebuyers' choice of conveyancing services, with nearly a third of purchasers selecting solicitors based on agent recommendations. A survey of 1,208 recent homeowners found 32% appointed conveyancers referred by their estate agent, making this the most common route to instruction. The findings emerge as some housing markets experience softer demand, intensifying competition among conveyancing firms for new business.

How Buyers Choose Their Conveyancer

Beyond estate agent referrals, personal recommendations from friends and family ranked as the second most popular method (25%), whilst only 19% of buyers found their conveyancer through online searches. Interestingly, recommendations appear to outweigh cost considerations for many purchasers. Among those comparing multiple firms, 38% prioritised professional referrals over other factors, compared to 20% who focused primarily on price and 11% swayed by online reviews.

The Importance of Agent Relationships

The research underscores how estate agents remain pivotal in the conveyancing process, even as digital comparison tools grow in popularity. Industry experts suggest these findings demonstrate why conveyancing firms might benefit from nurturing strong relationships with local agents. As one solicitor noted, agents typically recommend firms they trust to provide efficient, reliable service that reflects well on their own business and helps transactions proceed smoothly – particularly valuable in today's more challenging market conditions.

With estate agent recommendations continuing to shape consumer decisions significantly, conveyancers may wish to consider how maintaining these professional relationships could support their business growth amidst fluctuating market demand.

item.title: Agency warns punitive second-home taxes won’t free up housing
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Second-home tax hikes may fail to boost housing supply

Recent council tax premium increases on second homes across Scotland may do little to alleviate housing shortages, according to property experts. With local authorities implementing dramatically different surcharge rates – ranging from 225% in the Scottish Borders to 500% in Midlothian – analysts suggest these measures could become more about revenue generation than solving the housing crisis.

When combined with standard council tax, some second-home owners in Midlothian face potential bills reaching £28,000 annually on higher-value properties. Property professionals argue these punitive charges appear largely symbolic, given the limited number of affected properties. Midlothian Council, for instance, will only apply its maximum 600% levy to two properties under its graduated system.

Consultation concerns and policy effectiveness

Industry voices have raised concerns about the transparency of council consultations on these tax changes. Edinburgh's recent consultation exercise has drawn particular criticism, with observers noting the council's admission that the premium increase would proceed regardless of feedback. This has led some to question whether such consultations represent genuine engagement or merely box-ticking exercises.

Property market analysts highlight several potential issues with the current approach:

  • Limited impact on housing availability due to small numbers of affected properties
  • Potential negative effects on Scotland's reputation as a property investment destination
  • Questionable effectiveness compared to direct measures to increase housing supply

While policymakers present these tax hikes as solutions to housing shortages, critics argue they may simply divert attention from more substantive measures needed to address the root causes of Scotland's housing emergency. Homeowners and investors might wish to monitor how these policies develop across different local authorities in the coming months.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Second-home tax hikes may fail to boost housing supply

Recent council tax premium increases on second homes across Scotland may do little to alleviate housing shortages, according to property experts. With local authorities implementing dramatically different surcharge rates – ranging from 225% in the Scottish Borders to 500% in Midlothian – analysts suggest these measures could become more about revenue generation than solving the housing crisis.

When combined with standard council tax, some second-home owners in Midlothian face potential bills reaching £28,000 annually on higher-value properties. Property professionals argue these punitive charges appear largely symbolic, given the limited number of affected properties. Midlothian Council, for instance, will only apply its maximum 600% levy to two properties under its graduated system.

Consultation concerns and policy effectiveness

Industry voices have raised concerns about the transparency of council consultations on these tax changes. Edinburgh's recent consultation exercise has drawn particular criticism, with observers noting the council's admission that the premium increase would proceed regardless of feedback. This has led some to question whether such consultations represent genuine engagement or merely box-ticking exercises.

Property market analysts highlight several potential issues with the current approach:

  • Limited impact on housing availability due to small numbers of affected properties
  • Potential negative effects on Scotland's reputation as a property investment destination
  • Questionable effectiveness compared to direct measures to increase housing supply

While policymakers present these tax hikes as solutions to housing shortages, critics argue they may simply divert attention from more substantive measures needed to address the root causes of Scotland's housing emergency. Homeowners and investors might wish to monitor how these policies develop across different local authorities in the coming months.

item.title: Buyer demand remains weak as housing market struggles for momentum
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Housing Market Shows Tentative Signs of Stabilisation

The UK housing market remains subdued, though recent data suggests the pace of decline may be easing. According to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, buyer demand and agreed sales continue to weaken, reflecting ongoing economic uncertainty and higher mortgage costs. However, May marked the first month since January where conditions did not deteriorate further, potentially indicating the market is finding its floor.

New buyer enquiries held steady at a net balance of -34%, matching April's figure, whilst agreed sales remained negative at -37%. Though these metrics still point to challenging conditions, the lack of additional decline offers a glimmer of stability. Transaction times have lengthened considerably, with properties now taking an average of 21.5 weeks from listing to completion – the longest delay since records began in 2017.

Regional Variations and Rental Market Pressures

House prices continue to face downward pressure, with a net balance of -35% of surveyors reporting declines. The South East and East Anglia emerged as the weakest regions, whilst Northern Ireland bucked the trend with continued price growth. Looking ahead, sentiment appears cautiously optimistic, with the 12-month sales outlook turning slightly positive at +2%.

The rental market tells a different story, with demand outstripping supply. Tenant demand remained robust at +14%, whilst landlord instructions dwindled further to -28%. This imbalance has pushed rent expectations to +36%, their highest level in twelve months, suggesting further increases loom for tenants.

Experts Urge Caution Amid Economic Uncertainty

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research, noted: "Whilst the latest figures hint at stabilisation, key indicators remain in negative territory. It would be premature to declare this the start of a recovery." The recent dip in inflation provided temporary relief, but with energy costs still feeding through to prices, the Bank of England may yet implement further rate rises.

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals highlight the importance of careful financial planning. Mortgage borrowers might wish to explore fixed-rate options to hedge against potential future increases, whilst landlords may need to weigh rising rental yields against tighter regulations and higher borrowing costs.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Housing Market Shows Tentative Signs of Stabilisation

The UK housing market remains subdued, though recent data suggests the pace of decline may be easing. According to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, buyer demand and agreed sales continue to weaken, reflecting ongoing economic uncertainty and higher mortgage costs. However, May marked the first month since January where conditions did not deteriorate further, potentially indicating the market is finding its floor.

New buyer enquiries held steady at a net balance of -34%, matching April's figure, whilst agreed sales remained negative at -37%. Though these metrics still point to challenging conditions, the lack of additional decline offers a glimmer of stability. Transaction times have lengthened considerably, with properties now taking an average of 21.5 weeks from listing to completion – the longest delay since records began in 2017.

Regional Variations and Rental Market Pressures

House prices continue to face downward pressure, with a net balance of -35% of surveyors reporting declines. The South East and East Anglia emerged as the weakest regions, whilst Northern Ireland bucked the trend with continued price growth. Looking ahead, sentiment appears cautiously optimistic, with the 12-month sales outlook turning slightly positive at +2%.

The rental market tells a different story, with demand outstripping supply. Tenant demand remained robust at +14%, whilst landlord instructions dwindled further to -28%. This imbalance has pushed rent expectations to +36%, their highest level in twelve months, suggesting further increases loom for tenants.

Experts Urge Caution Amid Economic Uncertainty

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research, noted: "Whilst the latest figures hint at stabilisation, key indicators remain in negative territory. It would be premature to declare this the start of a recovery." The recent dip in inflation provided temporary relief, but with energy costs still feeding through to prices, the Bank of England may yet implement further rate rises.

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals highlight the importance of careful financial planning. Mortgage borrowers might wish to explore fixed-rate options to hedge against potential future increases, whilst landlords may need to weigh rising rental yields against tighter regulations and higher borrowing costs.

item.title: Navigating compliance charges under the Renters’ Rights reforms
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to the private rental sector, with many landlords now facing unexpected compliance costs. Reports suggest letting agents are charging between £200 and £400 for updating tenancy paperwork to meet new legal requirements, sparking debate about fairness and transparency in these additional fees.

Understanding the compliance costs

Landlords across the UK have voiced concerns about rising expenses linked to the Renters’ Rights reforms. Whilst some view these charges as excessive, industry experts note that legitimate costs may arise from:

  • Updating tenancy agreements and documentation
  • Implementing new procedures for tenant communications
  • Training staff on revised legal requirements
  • Distributing mandatory government information sheets

For larger agencies managing hundreds of properties, these changes require considerable administrative work. Letting agents also face their own regulatory obligations, with potential penalties for non-compliance adding pressure to ensure all processes meet the new standards.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

The key consideration for landlords isn’t whether fees can be charged, but whether they’re reasonable and properly justified. Property owners might wish to ask their agents to clarify:

  • Which specific services the fees cover
  • How the charges relate to their existing management agreement
  • The basis for the fee calculation
  • Whether the cost reflects the actual work involved

Many management contracts predate the Renters’ Rights Act, meaning they may not explicitly address these new compliance requirements. This grey area has led to some confusion about what constitutes fair additional charges versus unnecessary admin fees.

As the rental sector continues to adapt to these reforms, open communication between landlords and agents remains crucial to navigating compliance costs effectively. Both parties may benefit from reviewing agreements to ensure expectations around legislative changes are clearly outlined.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to the private rental sector, with many landlords now facing unexpected compliance costs. Reports suggest letting agents are charging between £200 and £400 for updating tenancy paperwork to meet new legal requirements, sparking debate about fairness and transparency in these additional fees.

Understanding the compliance costs

Landlords across the UK have voiced concerns about rising expenses linked to the Renters’ Rights reforms. Whilst some view these charges as excessive, industry experts note that legitimate costs may arise from:

  • Updating tenancy agreements and documentation
  • Implementing new procedures for tenant communications
  • Training staff on revised legal requirements
  • Distributing mandatory government information sheets

For larger agencies managing hundreds of properties, these changes require considerable administrative work. Letting agents also face their own regulatory obligations, with potential penalties for non-compliance adding pressure to ensure all processes meet the new standards.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

The key consideration for landlords isn’t whether fees can be charged, but whether they’re reasonable and properly justified. Property owners might wish to ask their agents to clarify:

  • Which specific services the fees cover
  • How the charges relate to their existing management agreement
  • The basis for the fee calculation
  • Whether the cost reflects the actual work involved

Many management contracts predate the Renters’ Rights Act, meaning they may not explicitly address these new compliance requirements. This grey area has led to some confusion about what constitutes fair additional charges versus unnecessary admin fees.

As the rental sector continues to adapt to these reforms, open communication between landlords and agents remains crucial to navigating compliance costs effectively. Both parties may benefit from reviewing agreements to ensure expectations around legislative changes are clearly outlined.

item.title: Rental stock shortages continue to drive rent growth
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices in the UK’s most affordable areas are surging at more than double the national rate, according to fresh market analysis. Despite tenant demand cooling to a six-year low, a chronic shortage of available properties continues to push rents upwards, particularly in lower-cost regions.

Affordable areas see sharpest rent rises

Recent data reveals rents are climbing by an average of 5% in areas where monthly payments fall below £750 – significantly outpacing the 2.1% national growth rate. Locations including Carlisle (9.1%), Kilmarnock (9.0%) and Halifax (6.5%) have experienced particularly steep increases, though their rents remain below UK averages.

The rental stock shortage appears widespread, with every UK region still showing 20-30% fewer available properties compared to pre-pandemic levels. Industry experts suggest this stems from reduced investment in the private rental sector, restricting tenant options and maintaining upward pricing pressure.

Mixed picture across UK regions

While affordability has improved nationally – with wages growing 4% against 2.1% rental inflation for 18 consecutive months – regional variations remain stark. Several major cities including Bournemouth, Nottingham and Birmingham have actually seen rents decline over the past year, as affordability limits and softer demand constrained landlords’ pricing power.

London presents a notable exception, being the sole region where tenant enquiries increased (up 6% annually). The capital’s average rents now stand at £2,206 per month, with growth accelerating to 2.2% – slightly above the national rate.

Market indicators suggest some relief for renters, with the average enquiries per listing dropping to 5.6 – the lowest since 2020 and well below 2022’s peak of 16. However, analysts caution that without significant new rental supply entering the market, current affordability improvements may prove temporary. Forecasts suggest rents could rise a further 2-3% before year-end.

For those considering their housing options, the regional variations highlight the importance of researching local market conditions. Whilst some areas show signs of easing pressure, others continue to experience significant rental growth, particularly in traditionally more affordable locations.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices in the UK’s most affordable areas are surging at more than double the national rate, according to fresh market analysis. Despite tenant demand cooling to a six-year low, a chronic shortage of available properties continues to push rents upwards, particularly in lower-cost regions.

Affordable areas see sharpest rent rises

Recent data reveals rents are climbing by an average of 5% in areas where monthly payments fall below £750 – significantly outpacing the 2.1% national growth rate. Locations including Carlisle (9.1%), Kilmarnock (9.0%) and Halifax (6.5%) have experienced particularly steep increases, though their rents remain below UK averages.

The rental stock shortage appears widespread, with every UK region still showing 20-30% fewer available properties compared to pre-pandemic levels. Industry experts suggest this stems from reduced investment in the private rental sector, restricting tenant options and maintaining upward pricing pressure.

Mixed picture across UK regions

While affordability has improved nationally – with wages growing 4% against 2.1% rental inflation for 18 consecutive months – regional variations remain stark. Several major cities including Bournemouth, Nottingham and Birmingham have actually seen rents decline over the past year, as affordability limits and softer demand constrained landlords’ pricing power.

London presents a notable exception, being the sole region where tenant enquiries increased (up 6% annually). The capital’s average rents now stand at £2,206 per month, with growth accelerating to 2.2% – slightly above the national rate.

Market indicators suggest some relief for renters, with the average enquiries per listing dropping to 5.6 – the lowest since 2020 and well below 2022’s peak of 16. However, analysts caution that without significant new rental supply entering the market, current affordability improvements may prove temporary. Forecasts suggest rents could rise a further 2-3% before year-end.

For those considering their housing options, the regional variations highlight the importance of researching local market conditions. Whilst some areas show signs of easing pressure, others continue to experience significant rental growth, particularly in traditionally more affordable locations.

item.title: Unlicensed landlord ordered to pay more than £10,000
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A landlord in Chester has been fined over £10,000 after being found guilty of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. Carl Southall, 60, was convicted at Mold Magistrates’ Court for 15 offences related to unlicensed property management activities across multiple locations. This case serves as a reminder of the importance of adhering to legal requirements in the rental market.

Details of the Offences

The offences took place in Shotton, Connah’s Quay, and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Southall was found to have collected rent from tenants at properties on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay, and Chester Road West in Queensferry, despite not holding the required licence. Initially, Southall pleaded guilty but later withdrew his pleas, leading the case to proceed to trial. Magistrates imposed fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to a total of £6,750.

Financial Penalties and Repayment

In addition to the fines, Southall was ordered to pay £1,750 in prosecution costs and a victim surcharge of £2,000. This brings the overall amount payable to £10,500. The court has mandated that this sum be repaid at a rate of £1,750 per month. This case highlights the financial consequences that can arise from failing to comply with property management regulations.

Landlords and property managers may wish to ensure they hold the appropriate licences and adhere to all legal requirements to avoid similar penalties. This case underscores the importance of staying informed about regulatory obligations in the rental property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A landlord in Chester has been fined over £10,000 after being found guilty of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. Carl Southall, 60, was convicted at Mold Magistrates’ Court for 15 offences related to unlicensed property management activities across multiple locations. This case serves as a reminder of the importance of adhering to legal requirements in the rental market.

Details of the Offences

The offences took place in Shotton, Connah’s Quay, and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Southall was found to have collected rent from tenants at properties on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay, and Chester Road West in Queensferry, despite not holding the required licence. Initially, Southall pleaded guilty but later withdrew his pleas, leading the case to proceed to trial. Magistrates imposed fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to a total of £6,750.

Financial Penalties and Repayment

In addition to the fines, Southall was ordered to pay £1,750 in prosecution costs and a victim surcharge of £2,000. This brings the overall amount payable to £10,500. The court has mandated that this sum be repaid at a rate of £1,750 per month. This case highlights the financial consequences that can arise from failing to comply with property management regulations.

Landlords and property managers may wish to ensure they hold the appropriate licences and adhere to all legal requirements to avoid similar penalties. This case underscores the importance of staying informed about regulatory obligations in the rental property market.

item.title: Rightmove set to be demoted from FTSE 100
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove, the UK's largest property portal, is poised to exit the FTSE 100 index later this month following a significant drop in its share price over the past year. The anticipated demotion to the FTSE 250, expected to take effect on 22nd June, marks the end of the company's long-standing position among Britain's blue-chip stocks. Housebuilder Berkeley Group Holdings may also face removal from the premier index in the upcoming quarterly reshuffle.

A challenging year for Rightmove

The potential index relegation comes after a turbulent period for Rightmove, with its share price declining by more than 40% in the last twelve months. Several factors have contributed to this downturn, including investor concerns about the company's £60m artificial intelligence investment programme and activist shareholder pressure. The situation was further complicated by a substantial £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year.

Rightmove's journey on the London Stock Exchange began in 2006 with a market valuation of approximately £425m. The company grew to become one of the UK's most valuable property businesses, reaching a peak market capitalisation of £6.7bn in 2021 during the pandemic-fuelled housing market boom.

Takeover approaches and market pressures

Last September, Rightmove rejected four acquisition bids from Australian property giant REA Group, with the highest offer valuing the company at £6.2bn. Following REA's decision to withdraw its interest, Rightmove's share price experienced a sharp decline. The stock currently trades well below REA's final offer price, with the company's market value now standing at around £3.3bn.

Market analysts suggest that Rightmove's current valuation reflects broader concerns about the UK property market's outlook, alongside company-specific challenges. The portal's heavy reliance on estate agent subscriptions makes it particularly sensitive to housing market fluctuations and changes in industry sentiment.

Whilst index changes primarily affect institutional investors, homeowners and property professionals might watch these developments as an indicator of market confidence in the UK's property sector. The coming months could prove crucial for Rightmove as it seeks to stabilise its position and demonstrate the value of its strategic investments.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove, the UK's largest property portal, is poised to exit the FTSE 100 index later this month following a significant drop in its share price over the past year. The anticipated demotion to the FTSE 250, expected to take effect on 22nd June, marks the end of the company's long-standing position among Britain's blue-chip stocks. Housebuilder Berkeley Group Holdings may also face removal from the premier index in the upcoming quarterly reshuffle.

A challenging year for Rightmove

The potential index relegation comes after a turbulent period for Rightmove, with its share price declining by more than 40% in the last twelve months. Several factors have contributed to this downturn, including investor concerns about the company's £60m artificial intelligence investment programme and activist shareholder pressure. The situation was further complicated by a substantial £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year.

Rightmove's journey on the London Stock Exchange began in 2006 with a market valuation of approximately £425m. The company grew to become one of the UK's most valuable property businesses, reaching a peak market capitalisation of £6.7bn in 2021 during the pandemic-fuelled housing market boom.

Takeover approaches and market pressures

Last September, Rightmove rejected four acquisition bids from Australian property giant REA Group, with the highest offer valuing the company at £6.2bn. Following REA's decision to withdraw its interest, Rightmove's share price experienced a sharp decline. The stock currently trades well below REA's final offer price, with the company's market value now standing at around £3.3bn.

Market analysts suggest that Rightmove's current valuation reflects broader concerns about the UK property market's outlook, alongside company-specific challenges. The portal's heavy reliance on estate agent subscriptions makes it particularly sensitive to housing market fluctuations and changes in industry sentiment.

Whilst index changes primarily affect institutional investors, homeowners and property professionals might watch these developments as an indicator of market confidence in the UK's property sector. The coming months could prove crucial for Rightmove as it seeks to stabilise its position and demonstrate the value of its strategic investments.

item.title: Buyer demand remains weak as housing market struggles for momentum
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market showed signs of stabilisation in May, according to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, though buyer demand and agreed sales remained weak. The data suggests that whilst the market continues to face challenges, the pace of decline may be slowing. This follows months of subdued activity driven by economic uncertainty and higher borrowing costs.

Buyer Demand and Sales Activity

New buyer enquiries recorded a net balance of -34%, unchanged from April, whilst agreed sales remained in negative territory at -37%. Although these figures indicate a challenging market, the lack of further deterioration suggests activity may be finding a floor. This marks the first month since January where demand did not worsen, offering a glimmer of hope for the months ahead.

However, transaction times continue to lengthen, with the average period from listing to completion now standing at 21.5 weeks. This is the longest delay recorded since RICS began collecting this data in 2017, highlighting ongoing inefficiencies in the market.

House Prices and Regional Trends

House prices remained under pressure, with a net balance of -35% of respondents reporting falls for the second consecutive month. Regional trends varied, with the South East and East Anglia experiencing the weakest price performance. In contrast, Northern Ireland continued to outperform, with surveyors reporting ongoing growth in this region.

Looking ahead, sentiment has improved slightly. Near-term sales expectations rose to -25%, marking an improvement on recent readings. The 12-month outlook moved into positive territory at +2%, suggesting some optimism that market conditions could strengthen over the coming year.

Rental Market Pressures

The rental market continues to face significant challenges, with tenant demand remaining positive at +14% whilst landlord instructions fell further to -28%. This imbalance between supply and demand is driving rental growth, with rent expectations increasing to +36%, their strongest level in a year. This suggests tenants may face further increases in rental costs in the months ahead.

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research and Analysis, commented: “The latest survey data suggest the recent downturn in activity may be beginning to stabilise, with several key indicators broadly holding steady. However, as they remain in negative territory, it would be premature to interpret this as the start of a recovery.”

Whilst the housing market may be showing tentative signs of stabilisation, homeowners and prospective buyers are likely to remain cautious in the face of ongoing economic pressures and the potential for further interest rate rises.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market showed signs of stabilisation in May, according to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, though buyer demand and agreed sales remained weak. The data suggests that whilst the market continues to face challenges, the pace of decline may be slowing. This follows months of subdued activity driven by economic uncertainty and higher borrowing costs.

Buyer Demand and Sales Activity

New buyer enquiries recorded a net balance of -34%, unchanged from April, whilst agreed sales remained in negative territory at -37%. Although these figures indicate a challenging market, the lack of further deterioration suggests activity may be finding a floor. This marks the first month since January where demand did not worsen, offering a glimmer of hope for the months ahead.

However, transaction times continue to lengthen, with the average period from listing to completion now standing at 21.5 weeks. This is the longest delay recorded since RICS began collecting this data in 2017, highlighting ongoing inefficiencies in the market.

House Prices and Regional Trends

House prices remained under pressure, with a net balance of -35% of respondents reporting falls for the second consecutive month. Regional trends varied, with the South East and East Anglia experiencing the weakest price performance. In contrast, Northern Ireland continued to outperform, with surveyors reporting ongoing growth in this region.

Looking ahead, sentiment has improved slightly. Near-term sales expectations rose to -25%, marking an improvement on recent readings. The 12-month outlook moved into positive territory at +2%, suggesting some optimism that market conditions could strengthen over the coming year.

Rental Market Pressures

The rental market continues to face significant challenges, with tenant demand remaining positive at +14% whilst landlord instructions fell further to -28%. This imbalance between supply and demand is driving rental growth, with rent expectations increasing to +36%, their strongest level in a year. This suggests tenants may face further increases in rental costs in the months ahead.

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research and Analysis, commented: “The latest survey data suggest the recent downturn in activity may be beginning to stabilise, with several key indicators broadly holding steady. However, as they remain in negative territory, it would be premature to interpret this as the start of a recovery.”

Whilst the housing market may be showing tentative signs of stabilisation, homeowners and prospective buyers are likely to remain cautious in the face of ongoing economic pressures and the potential for further interest rate rises.

item.title: Navigating compliance charges under the Renters’ Rights reforms
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
