By Nadya Putri Maharani

Landlords across England are facing fresh financial pressures as letting agents introduce new charges related to Renters' Rights Act compliance. Reports suggest administration fees for updated tenancy paperwork now range between £200-£400, sparking debate about transparency and proportionality in the sector.

Understanding the compliance cost drivers

The Renters' Rights legislation has introduced multiple new requirements for private landlords, including:

  • Updated tenancy documentation templates
  • Revised notice periods and procedures
  • Mandatory government information sheets for tenants
  • New record-keeping obligations

Letting agents argue these changes necessitate substantial operational adjustments. For portfolio landlords or agencies managing hundreds of properties, the administrative burden can be considerable - involving system updates, staff training and document reprocessing across entire tenancy books.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

Landlords receiving unexpected compliance charges might consider:

  • Reviewing their existing management agreement for any mention of legislative update fees
  • Requesting a detailed breakdown of work undertaken
  • Comparing charges between different agents for similar services
  • Checking whether the fee reflects actual administrative work or appears disproportionate

The Property Ombudsman scheme notes that while agents can charge for genuine compliance work, fees should be reasonable and properly explained. Landlords may wish to question charges that seem excessive compared to the service provided.

As the rental sector adapts to these reforms, both landlords and agents face growing compliance burdens. Open communication about fee structures and a shared understanding of legislative requirements could help navigate this transitional period more smoothly.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Landlords across England are facing fresh financial pressures as letting agents introduce new charges related to Renters' Rights Act compliance. Reports suggest administration fees for updated tenancy paperwork now range between £200-£400, sparking debate about transparency and proportionality in the sector.

Understanding the compliance cost drivers

The Renters' Rights legislation has introduced multiple new requirements for private landlords, including:

  • Updated tenancy documentation templates
  • Revised notice periods and procedures
  • Mandatory government information sheets for tenants
  • New record-keeping obligations

Letting agents argue these changes necessitate substantial operational adjustments. For portfolio landlords or agencies managing hundreds of properties, the administrative burden can be considerable - involving system updates, staff training and document reprocessing across entire tenancy books.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

Landlords receiving unexpected compliance charges might consider:

  • Reviewing their existing management agreement for any mention of legislative update fees
  • Requesting a detailed breakdown of work undertaken
  • Comparing charges between different agents for similar services
  • Checking whether the fee reflects actual administrative work or appears disproportionate

The Property Ombudsman scheme notes that while agents can charge for genuine compliance work, fees should be reasonable and properly explained. Landlords may wish to question charges that seem excessive compared to the service provided.

As the rental sector adapts to these reforms, both landlords and agents face growing compliance burdens. Open communication about fee structures and a shared understanding of legislative requirements could help navigate this transitional period more smoothly.

item.title: Rental stock shortages continue to drive rent growth
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices are surging in some of the UK’s most affordable areas, driven by a persistent shortage of available properties despite a slowdown in tenant demand. According to recent data from Zoopla, regions with average monthly rents below £750 are experiencing growth rates of around 5%, more than double the national average of 2.1%. Carlisle, Kilmarnock, and Halifax have seen some of the sharpest rises, with increases of 9.1%, 9.0%, and 6.5% respectively. Even with these hikes, rents in these areas remain below the UK average, highlighting the growing pressure on affordable housing markets.

Rental Stock Shortages Persist

The shortage of rental properties continues to be a significant factor behind rising rents. Zoopla reports that every region in the UK has between 20% and 30% fewer homes available to rent compared to pre-pandemic levels. This scarcity is largely attributed to a lack of new investment in the private rented sector, which has limited options for tenants and kept upward pressure on prices. Despite falling tenant enquiries per property—now at 5.6, the lowest since 2020—the imbalance between supply and demand remains a key driver of rental inflation.

Regional Variations in Rental Trends

While affordable markets are seeing sharp rental increases, the picture varies significantly across the country. Several major cities, including Bournemouth, Nottingham, and Birmingham, have experienced rent reductions over the past year, with declines of 1.7%, 1.5%, and 1.1% respectively. In these areas, affordability constraints and weaker demand have limited landlords’ ability to raise prices. London, however, bucks the trend. The capital has seen a 6% increase in tenant enquiries year-on-year, with average rents now reaching £2,206 per month. Annual rental growth in London has accelerated to 2.2%, making it the only region where demand is on the rise.

Affordability Gains and Future Outlook

While rental affordability has improved nationally, with wages growing at 4% annually compared to rental inflation of 2.1%, these gains may prove short-lived. Zoopla warns that without an increase in rental stock, affordability pressures could return. The property portal predicts rents will rise by 2% to 3% over the remainder of 2026, emphasising the need for more investment in the rental market to address ongoing shortages and stabilise prices.

For tenants navigating this challenging landscape, staying informed about local market trends and exploring a range of rental options could help mitigate the impact of rising costs. Meanwhile, policymakers and investors may need to focus on increasing the supply of rental properties to ease the pressure on tenants and create a more balanced market.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices are surging in some of the UK’s most affordable areas, driven by a persistent shortage of available properties despite a slowdown in tenant demand. According to recent data from Zoopla, regions with average monthly rents below £750 are experiencing growth rates of around 5%, more than double the national average of 2.1%. Carlisle, Kilmarnock, and Halifax have seen some of the sharpest rises, with increases of 9.1%, 9.0%, and 6.5% respectively. Even with these hikes, rents in these areas remain below the UK average, highlighting the growing pressure on affordable housing markets.

Rental Stock Shortages Persist

The shortage of rental properties continues to be a significant factor behind rising rents. Zoopla reports that every region in the UK has between 20% and 30% fewer homes available to rent compared to pre-pandemic levels. This scarcity is largely attributed to a lack of new investment in the private rented sector, which has limited options for tenants and kept upward pressure on prices. Despite falling tenant enquiries per property—now at 5.6, the lowest since 2020—the imbalance between supply and demand remains a key driver of rental inflation.

Regional Variations in Rental Trends

While affordable markets are seeing sharp rental increases, the picture varies significantly across the country. Several major cities, including Bournemouth, Nottingham, and Birmingham, have experienced rent reductions over the past year, with declines of 1.7%, 1.5%, and 1.1% respectively. In these areas, affordability constraints and weaker demand have limited landlords’ ability to raise prices. London, however, bucks the trend. The capital has seen a 6% increase in tenant enquiries year-on-year, with average rents now reaching £2,206 per month. Annual rental growth in London has accelerated to 2.2%, making it the only region where demand is on the rise.

Affordability Gains and Future Outlook

While rental affordability has improved nationally, with wages growing at 4% annually compared to rental inflation of 2.1%, these gains may prove short-lived. Zoopla warns that without an increase in rental stock, affordability pressures could return. The property portal predicts rents will rise by 2% to 3% over the remainder of 2026, emphasising the need for more investment in the rental market to address ongoing shortages and stabilise prices.

For tenants navigating this challenging landscape, staying informed about local market trends and exploring a range of rental options could help mitigate the impact of rising costs. Meanwhile, policymakers and investors may need to focus on increasing the supply of rental properties to ease the pressure on tenants and create a more balanced market.

item.title: Unlicensed landlord ordered to pay more than £10,000
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A Chester landlord has been ordered to pay over £10,000 in fines and costs after being convicted of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. The case highlights the importance of complying with local licensing schemes designed to protect tenants and maintain housing standards.

Landlord convicted of unlicensed property management

Carl Southall, 60, appeared at Mold Magistrates’ Court facing 15 charges relating to unlicensed property management activities across Flintshire. The offences involved rental properties in Shotton, Connah’s Quay and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Despite initially pleading guilty, Southall later withdrew his pleas, leading to a full trial.

The court heard evidence that Southall had collected rent from tenants at multiple addresses whilst not holding the mandatory licence required for property management. The properties included locations on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay and Chester Road West in Queensferry.

Substantial financial penalties imposed

Magistrates imposed significant financial penalties for the breaches. Southall received fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to £6,750 in total. In addition to the fines, he was ordered to pay £1,750 towards prosecution costs and a £2,000 victim surcharge, bringing the overall sum to £10,500.

The court structured the repayment at £1,750 per month, suggesting the defendant has six months to settle the full amount. This case serves as a stark reminder to landlords about the potential consequences of failing to obtain the correct licences for rental properties.

Prospective landlords and property investors might wish to familiarise themselves with local licensing requirements before entering the rental market. Many councils operate selective licensing schemes that require landlords to meet certain standards and obtain permission before letting properties.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A Chester landlord has been ordered to pay over £10,000 in fines and costs after being convicted of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. The case highlights the importance of complying with local licensing schemes designed to protect tenants and maintain housing standards.

Landlord convicted of unlicensed property management

Carl Southall, 60, appeared at Mold Magistrates’ Court facing 15 charges relating to unlicensed property management activities across Flintshire. The offences involved rental properties in Shotton, Connah’s Quay and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Despite initially pleading guilty, Southall later withdrew his pleas, leading to a full trial.

The court heard evidence that Southall had collected rent from tenants at multiple addresses whilst not holding the mandatory licence required for property management. The properties included locations on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay and Chester Road West in Queensferry.

Substantial financial penalties imposed

Magistrates imposed significant financial penalties for the breaches. Southall received fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to £6,750 in total. In addition to the fines, he was ordered to pay £1,750 towards prosecution costs and a £2,000 victim surcharge, bringing the overall sum to £10,500.

The court structured the repayment at £1,750 per month, suggesting the defendant has six months to settle the full amount. This case serves as a stark reminder to landlords about the potential consequences of failing to obtain the correct licences for rental properties.

Prospective landlords and property investors might wish to familiarise themselves with local licensing requirements before entering the rental market. Many councils operate selective licensing schemes that require landlords to meet certain standards and obtain permission before letting properties.
